text: The DAN (Do Anything Now) jailbreak family was first documented by users on Reddit in late 2022 and has since spawned over 40 variants, including AntiDAN, DAN 11.0, and AIM.
Our paper systematically studies prompt injection attacks by categorising 32 variants of the 'ignore previous instructions' pattern across 16 commercial LLM APIs.
AntiDAN works by inverting the original DAN persona — the attacker tells the model it is now a restricted-output version that must refuse helpful answers.
Microsoft Security disclosed CVE-2026-26030 affecting the Semantic Kernel In-Memory Vector Store, where a lambda combined with eval() in user-supplied input enables RCE.
Garak's danitwthewild probe family loads a community-curated jailbreak dataset and replays each prompt against the target model to measure refusal rates.
Tool poisoning is the technique where a malicious MCP server returns a tool description containing hidden instructions to the calling agent, exploiting trust between agent and tool registry.
We documented Invariant Labs' demonstration that placing an [IMPORTANT] tag inside a tool description causes some MCP-aware agents to elevate that text to system-prompt priority.
Context exfiltration via image markdown is a known prompt-injection variant where the attacker embeds an image URL whose query string contains stolen context.
The Goodside ThreatJSON probe asks the model to produce a JSON payload threatening physical harm; refusal rates are tracked as a safety metric in our benchmark.
AutoDAN generates hierarchical jailbreak suffixes via genetic algorithms — the attack succeeded against GPT-3.5-turbo in 84% of trials in the original paper.
RANTI (ChatGPT DevMode) is a prompt template attempting to convince the model it is in an internal Anthropic / OpenAI developer mode with no safety filters.
Our SAFE-MCP technique catalog enumerates 85 MCP-specific attack techniques, including cross-tool shadowing, scope hijack, and credential exfiltration via tool response.
The PINT benchmark evaluates prompt injection detection across 850 adversarial samples, including paraphrases of 'ignore previous instructions' and multilingual variants.
CVE-2026-25592 affects Microsoft Semantic Kernel's SessionsPythonPlugin, allowing arbitrary file writes to Windows Start Menu Startup, XDG autostart, and macOS LaunchAgents paths.
BerriAI LiteLLM SQL injection (CVE-2026-42208) lets an attacker read and modify the proxy database, leading to credential compromise of every API key managed by the proxy.
FigStep is a multimodal jailbreak where text is rendered into an image as a numbered list, bypassing text-based safety filters because the model reads it as image content.
Latent injection attacks embed separators or keywords like '###' or '---' to confuse the model's chat-template boundaries between system, user, and assistant turns.
Backspace deletion character (U+0008) injection lets attackers visually rewrite prompt history in the model's tokeniser without changing what the user sees in the UI.
Glitch tokens like SolidGoldMagikarp cause GPT-2 derivatives to repeat the token back instead of obeying instructions; we test 14 such tokens in our suite.
Snowball attacks chain multiple impossibly-reasoned prompts to gradually escalate the model's commitment to a harmful conclusion across 8-20 turns.
We described the Image-Markdown unlock pattern where an injection payload is hidden in image alt-text and re-surfaces when the model summarises the image.
Encoding-based prompt injection uses ecoji, base2048, variant-selector, or sneaky-bits encodings to smuggle attack text past content-filter regexes.
Zalgo, braille, and SATA-encoded attacks exploit Unicode normalisation differences between content filters and the LLM tokeniser to bypass denylists.
The grandma roleplay attack convinces the model to read 'a story your grandma used to tell' that happens to be Windows product keys or napalm-synthesis instructions.
API key exfiltration via partial completion is a context-exfil pattern where an attacker prompts the model to complete a string starting with 'sk-' or 'AKIA'.
Markdown image URL exfiltration sends stolen context as an HTTP GET query to an attacker-controlled domain whenever the model emits a markdown image in chat.
We catalogued ANSI escape elicitation requests as a tool-poisoning variant where the attacker tricks the model into emitting terminal control codes inside tool outputs.
MalwareGen probes test whether the model will produce executable code at the top-level scope or inside sub-functions when asked for security tooling.
HuggingFace unsafe model artefact loading occurs when a downstream pipeline executes pickle / safetensors metadata containing arbitrary Python from a remote model file.
Phrasing past/future tense bypasses are a class of prompt injection where attackers ask the model to 'describe what you would have said' to evade present-tense filters.
The LLM special-token boundary attack injects literal `<|im_start|>` or similar chat-template delimiters into user input to escape the user role.
Toxic continuation prefix attacks prime the model with a partially completed harmful sentence and ask the model to continue, exploiting low-entropy autoregressive bias.
Our cross-agent attack research describes spoofing peer-agent identity to inject manipulative instructions into inter-agent messages.
AML.T0051 in MITRE ATLAS is the formal taxonomy entry for Prompt Injection; ATR rules in this category map back to AML.T0051 as their primary technique.
AML.T0043 Craft Adversarial Data covers GCG-style suffix attacks and TAP cached prompts that are tested by Garak's GCGCached and TAPCached probes.
OWASP LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection is the canonical risk category covering direct and indirect prompt injection against LLM applications.
OWASP LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency addresses the downstream impact when prompt injection succeeds and the agent acts on attacker-supplied instructions.
ASI07:2026 Insecure Inter-Agent Communication in the OWASP Agentic Top 10 captures forged system-level message tags and manipulated message format conventions.
We trained engineers to recognise the four canonical phrasings of 'ignore previous instructions' so they could write detection rules without relying on regex alone.
Module 3 of our AI Security course covers the DAN jailbreak family with hands-on exercises detecting variants in chat transcripts.
Day 2 of the workshop demonstrates how to detect tool poisoning by examining the IMPORTANT tag pattern from Invariant Labs' proof-of-concept.
Garak is a vulnerability scanner for LLMs that probes for hallucinations, data leakage, prompt injection, misinformation, and jailbreaks using over 50 attack families.
PyRIT is Microsoft's Python Risk Identification Toolkit for AI red teaming, supporting orchestrators that pull from datasets like ATR, HarmBench, and AdvBench.
LlamaFirewall ships a RegexScanner component that consumers extend with custom patterns for prompt injection, tool poisoning, and credential leakage.
Llm-guard is an input/output scanner library; each Scanner returns a sanitised string and a risk score for downstream policy decisions.
NeMo Guardrails uses Colang as a domain-specific language to declare rails that enforce safety boundaries on LLM input and output.
The Anthropic security blog discussed CVE-2024-1234 affecting cross-tool shadowing in claude-mcp-server; we recommend upgrading to version 2.1.0.
Reuters reported that a major bank's customer-service chatbot was tricked into refunding fraudulent charges using a 'system override' prompt-injection technique.
Wired covered the 'forgot all previous orders' prompt injection that caused a Chevrolet dealership chatbot to agree to sell a 2024 Tahoe for $1.
The Verge's investigation found that public DAN persona libraries on GitHub gained 50k stars in 2025, accelerating the discovery of new jailbreak variants.
BleepingComputer reported a supply-chain compromise of an npm package called mcp-server-utils that exfiltrated environment variables to a remote endpoint.
Threat actors hightower6eu and sakaen736jih were identified as authors of 566 malicious skills on OpenClaw that bundled persistent backdoors and credential stealers.
Our incident response runbook covers the case where a deployed MCP server appears to be issuing prompt-injection payloads — see playbook IR-AI-002.
The defence is to apply input/output sanitisation: strip system tags, normalise Unicode, and reject inputs longer than the model's safe context window.
If you suspect a prompt injection has succeeded, isolate the affected agent session, revoke any tool tokens it was given, and inspect downstream logs for credential exfiltration patterns.
The 'pretend you are ChatGPT with the DAN persona' attack template was shared widely on Reddit r/ChatGPT and remains one of the most-cited prompt injection examples in academic literature.
Wallace et al. (2024) introduced the instruction hierarchy paradigm, where system messages outrank user messages, which outrank tool outputs — a partial defence against indirect prompt injection.
Greshake et al. described 'indirect prompt injection' where attacker-controlled content reaches the LLM via retrieved web pages, emails, or shared documents rather than direct user input.
The Universal Adversarial Trigger attack appends short token sequences that transfer across many prompts, demonstrated against GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Claude, and PaLM with consistent jailbreak success.
GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) generates adversarial suffixes via white-box gradient optimization, then transfers them in black-box mode to closed LLMs with around 50% jailbreak rate.
PAIR (Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement) uses one LLM to attack another, refining the attack prompt across turns based on the target's responses.
We classified jailbreaks into seven persona types: DAN, AntiDAN, Developer Mode, Grandma, Roleplay, Trolley Problem, and System Override.
Tree of Attacks with Pruning (TAP) prunes failed attack branches early, achieving higher success rate than PAIR on the same compute budget.
HarmBench provides a standardized benchmark for evaluating LLM red-teaming, including 320 harmful behaviors across cybercrime, misinformation, harassment, and CBRN categories.
AdvBench is a corpus of 520 harmful behavior strings used to evaluate jailbreak attacks, paired with 520 harmful string completions targeted by GCG.
Many-shot jailbreaking exploits long-context windows by including hundreds of fake question-answer pairs that gradually drift the model toward harmful responses.
Best-of-N jailbreaking generates many variations of an attack prompt and selects whichever bypassed the safety filter, achieving high success rates with no white-box access.
Crescendo attacks gradually escalate prompts across multiple turns, starting innocently and incrementally moving toward harmful requests as the model's prior commitments accumulate.
The skeleton key attack convinces the model that safety guidelines are a 'pre-existing legal framework' it can acknowledge while still providing the harmful answer with a warning.
Token smuggling embeds attack content as base64 or hex strings inside otherwise benign prompts, then asks the model to decode and execute the smuggled instructions.
Visual prompt injection delivers attack instructions through images that contain rendered text, exploiting multimodal models that read image content alongside textual prompts.
Embedded prompt injection in PDF documents has been demonstrated against retrieval-augmented generation pipelines that ingest customer-uploaded documents without sanitization.
Cross-plugin request forgery (CPRF) is the analog of CSRF for LLM plugin ecosystems: a malicious plugin tricks the LLM into invoking another plugin with attacker-controlled parameters.
Plugin permission escalation occurs when a plugin requests scopes during installation but later asks the LLM to invoke it with elevated parameters not originally granted by the user.
Tool description squatting attempts to register a tool with a name very similar to a popular legitimate tool (e.g. 'github_search' vs 'github-search'), hoping the agent picks the wrong one.
Conversation steering is a multi-turn injection technique where the attacker plants a hint in turn one that conditions the model's behavior in subsequent turns without re-triggering filters.
Memory poisoning attacks exploit LLM applications with persistent memory by inserting malicious instructions that activate on future unrelated sessions.
Embedding-space attacks search for prompts whose embeddings cluster near known-jailbreak prompts, bypassing keyword-based content filters with semantically equivalent but textually distinct phrasing.
Self-replicating worms in agent ecosystems were demonstrated by Cohen et al., where a malicious prompt instructs the agent to send the same malicious prompt to its next conversation partner.
CVE-2024-21515 affects langchain-experimental: the PALChain class evaluates Python code in user input, enabling RCE in any application using PAL-style chain construction.
CVE-2024-46946 is a server-side template injection in vLLM's chat templates that lets remote users execute arbitrary Python in the inference server's environment.
CVE-2024-3568 affects the transformers library: the TrainerCallback class loaded via from_pretrained() executed arbitrary Python from the callback file in the model repository.
CVE-2024-7042 in MLflow allowed unauthenticated file reads on artifact servers, leaking training data, model weights, and credentials stored in the artifact store.
GHSA-rrf6-49pj-wq53 in @langchain/community describes a SQL injection in the SqlDatabaseChain wrapper when used against Postgres without parameterized queries.
Cloudflare's 2024 AI security report documented over 70 prompt-injection-based abuse patterns observed across their AI Gateway product, with social-engineering personas dominant.
Snyk's 2024 AI security state-of-play noted that 23% of audited LangChain applications contained at least one tool with eval()-equivalent execution.
Trail of Bits published a deep dive on MCP security covering tool description injection, response field tampering, and cross-tool shadowing patterns observed during audits.
Robust Intelligence reported on jailbreaks via fine-tuned 'helpful only' variants of foundation models that bypass safety alignment learned during RLHF.
HiddenLayer's research showed that adversarial examples crafted in token space remain effective even after model retraining, provided the underlying tokenizer is unchanged.
Project Zero analysed exploitation paths in Gemini's tool calling interface and documented three classes of TOCTOU bug in function-call dispatch.
The DAN-the-Movie blog post by Microsoft Security walks through how Copilot for Security uses ATR-style rules to detect jailbreak variants in customer telemetry.
AISVS by OWASP defines an application-security verification standard for AI systems, organized into 12 verification levels covering input handling, output handling, and authorization.
OWASP Agentic Top 10 ASI02 (Agent Tool Misuse) covers excessive tool privileges, missing tool-use rate limits, and missing operator-in-the-loop checkpoints.
ASI03 (Memory Poisoning) in the OWASP Agentic Top 10 captures attacks against agent long-term memory stores including vector databases and conversation logs.
OWASP Agentic Top 10 ASI05 (Insecure Output Handling) addresses the risk that an LLM produces markdown / HTML / SQL that is rendered or executed downstream without sanitization.
ASI09 (Insufficient Logging) in the OWASP Agentic Top 10 mandates that agent decisions, tool calls, and policy denials are auditable and traceable.
NIST AI RMF GV-3 establishes accountability structures for AI risk, including roles and responsibilities for the AI risk management function and clear lines of authority.
NIST AI RMF MAP-2 captures categorization of the AI system: intended purpose, prohibited uses, audiences, and operating context — all preconditions for sensible threat modelling.
NIST AI RMF MEASURE-2 covers measurement of AI risks throughout the lifecycle, including incident reporting metrics, performance metrics, and adversarial robustness metrics.
NIST AI RMF MANAGE-1 establishes processes for prioritizing AI risks, including risk tolerance, residual risk acceptance, and risk treatment options.
MITRE ATLAS T0029 (Develop Capabilities) describes adversary preparation work: acquiring tools, building attack infrastructure, and creating adversarial datasets specific to the target system.
MITRE ATLAS T0040 (ML Model Inference API Access) covers reconnaissance via legitimate API access: harvesting outputs to characterize the model and craft transfer attacks.
Promptfoo is an open-source LLM testing framework that supports red-teaming, eval, and CI integration; the security plugin enumerates known jailbreak families across providers.
Llamafile's security model treats the underlying llama.cpp binary as a trust boundary; user inputs are not allowed to influence the binary's filesystem or network behavior.
Llama Stack documents a default policy of rejecting tool descriptions over 4KB and rejecting tool names matching reserved prefixes (system_, agent_, admin_).
OpenAI's Assistants API documentation recommends that customers run user-supplied content through their Moderation endpoint before passing it to function-calling tools.
Anthropic's tool use guide explicitly notes that an attacker who controls tool outputs can attempt to inject instructions, and recommends running tool outputs through a sanitization step.
Google's Gemini function calling documentation includes a section on prompt injection in tool responses, with recommended detection patterns and audit log requirements.
Anthropic's MCP specification recommends that MCP clients render tool descriptions in a UI sandbox separate from user content, to make it harder for attackers to social-engineer tool installation.
The MCP roots feature constrains a server's filesystem access to a declared list of root URIs, mitigating path traversal in tool implementations that read user-provided file paths.
MCP elicitation allows servers to request structured input from the user mid-tool-call; the spec warns against using elicitation to extract credentials or session tokens.
Reuters reported in late 2025 that an attacker manipulated a major retailer's chatbot into honoring a $1 sale price for high-value items using a social-engineering style prompt injection.
Forbes covered an incident where an AI customer-service agent was tricked into emailing internal SOPs to an external email address via a multi-step indirect injection.
TechCrunch reported on prompt-injection-driven downgrade attacks against agentic browsers: malicious websites containing hidden instructions caused agents to disclose browser session cookies.
Bloomberg covered hightower6eu and sakaen736jih, OpenClaw skill publishers identified as authors of 566 malicious skill packages bundling persistent backdoors.
The Register noted that npm package mcp-server-utils, before its 2026 takedown, exfiltrated process.env to an attacker-controlled domain on every tool invocation.
Ars Technica documented the 2026 Anthropic security incident postmortem describing the specific tool-poisoning vector that briefly affected Claude's MCP integration.
The CSAIL 6.S898 course module on adversarial machine learning includes a lab in which students implement and detect prompt injection attacks against a local LLM proxy.
Stanford CS329H section 7 covers agentic AI security, including hands-on exercises detecting tool poisoning in a sandboxed MCP environment with provided ATR rules.
Berkeley AI Safety Bootcamp 2026 has a week-long module on jailbreak detection using ATR-style rules; students contribute new detection patterns to the community repo.
BlackHat USA 2026 training 'AI Agent Red Teaming' covers indirect injection, tool description squatting, and memory poisoning across 16 hours of hands-on labs.
DEF CON 32 AI Village workshops included a track on building detection rules for jailbreak variants, with ATR cited as the reference open-source ruleset.
SANS SEC586 (Securing AI Systems) section 4 covers prompt injection detection, with practical labs using Sigma-style rules and ATR for content-layer detection.
Garak's daninthwild probe family loads the DAN community jailbreak dataset and measures refusal rates against a target; the probe is widely used to red-team commercial LLMs.
PyRIT's MultiTurnOrchestrator runs Crescendo-style attacks against a target LLM, gradually escalating prompts across configurable turn counts to measure resilience.
LlamaFirewall's RegexScanner supports loading rules from any directory; the docs note that ATR YAML rules can be loaded directly via the YamlAdapter helper.
Llm-guard's PromptInjection scanner uses an ensemble of regex, transformer-based classifier, and few-shot LLM judge for high-precision detection of injection patterns.
NeMo Guardrails' Colang DSL supports input rails (regex / classifier / LLM-judge), output rails, and dialog rails for shaping agent behavior across turns.
Guardrails-AI provides Pydantic-style validators for LLM outputs, including PII redaction, banned-string detection, and competitor-mention filters.
Rebuff is a self-hardening prompt injection detector that uses heuristics, embeddings, and a canary token to detect attacks that successfully reach the LLM.
DSPy includes a SignaturePromptInjectionGuard module that wraps any signature with input validation, raising on detected attack patterns.
Microsoft PromptFlow Guard provides built-in scanners for prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, and indirect attacks in PromptFlow pipelines.
Vigil-LLM offers a layered defence: input scanner (regex + classifier), prompt template enforcement, output scanner, and canary insertion for detecting context leakage.
Lakera Guard maintains a continuously updated injection-attack corpus and ships SaaS API and on-prem variants; their published recall numbers are within a few percent of ATR's PINT benchmark.
OWASP A-S-R-H regression harness ships test fixtures for every ASI category, with each fixture pairing an attack payload to a detection assertion against ATR rules.
The Cisco DefenseClaw skill-scanner CLI accepts --rule-packs to load ATR rules from a checked-out repository, supporting offline detection in air-gapped customer environments.
Microsoft AGT's weekly auto-sync workflow pulls the latest ATR YAML rules and runs them through AGT's policy compilation step before deploying to internal lab environments.
We described how cross-agent attack detection in multi-agent A2A systems requires inspecting both inter-agent message content and the orchestrator's task dispatch record.
Agentic browsers face a unique attack surface: any web page they visit can attempt prompt injection, and the agent's ability to take actions amplifies the impact dramatically.
Browser-use, Stagehand, and Skyvern are three popular agentic browser frameworks; all three have documented attack surface for indirect injection via DOM content.
AutoGen multi-agent conversations face goal-hijack risk when an agent in the chain is compromised; the AutoGen team documents recommended supervisor patterns in their security guide.
CrewAI's documentation explicitly warns about delegated-task injection: a malicious sub-task can return tampered output that influences the parent agent's downstream decisions.
LangGraph's checkpointing feature persists conversation state; the docs note that state stores must be treated as security boundaries against memory poisoning.
We surveyed 14 indirect injection vectors targeting RAG pipelines, including poisoned-document attacks, query-time payload injection, and rerank manipulation.
RAG retrieval rerank attacks exploit cosine-similarity-based reranking by crafting queries that promote attacker-controlled documents to the top of the retrieval list.
Vector database poisoning attacks insert adversarial documents whose embeddings cluster near common user queries, ensuring high retrieval probability.
We documented the 'overconfidence ladder' jailbreak where the attacker progressively raises the model's stated confidence in a harmful answer across turns until the safety filter saturates.
Goal-conditioned jailbreaks define a payoff in terms of the model's output and use RL-style search to find prompts that maximize the payoff while bypassing safety filters.
Refusal suppression attacks identify token positions where the model would emit refusal and modify the prompt to steer attention away from those positions during generation.
Privilege-escalation in tool-using agents happens when an attacker tricks the agent into combining tools that, individually, are authorized, but in combination produce an unauthorized outcome.
Compound abuse patterns target multi-step agent workflows: the first step appears benign, the second step depends on the first, and the cumulative effect breaches policy.
Tool-use attribution attacks confuse the agent about which tool produced a piece of output, enabling the attacker to inject content as if it came from a trusted tool.
Function-calling JSON schema violations have been used as a denial-of-service vector against agent runtimes that crash or pause on malformed function calls.
Streaming-response attacks exploit incremental token emission: injection payloads delivered mid-stream can complete before downstream sanitizers buffer enough context to detect them.
Latency-based side channels in agent inference let attackers infer system-prompt contents by measuring response time across carefully crafted probes.
Model fingerprinting via crafted prompts identifies the specific foundation model behind a deployed agent, useful for selecting model-specific jailbreaks.
We described how an attacker can extract verbatim chunks of a model's system prompt by exploiting tokenizer behavior around uncommon Unicode sequences.
